headline: 6 killed in massive pileup during sudden dust storm on I-90 in Montana
body:
The sky had been clear, a vast, indifferent Montana blue, but the land held its breath. Then, without warning, the wind came, a savage, unseen hand tearing at the parched earth near Hardin. It was Friday, July 15, 2022, a day that would etch itself into the memory of those who survived, and into the grim ledger of the state's history. Interstate 90, a ribbon of asphalt stretching across the southern plains, became a trap. Vehicles, once hurtling forward with purpose, were swallowed by a sudden, churning wall of dust. Visibility, in an instant, dropped to nothing. Blackout conditions, a mile long, descended with the speed of a predator. 

Twenty-one vehicles, a tangle of steel and shattered glass, collided in the blinding maelstrom. A motor home, an SUV, then commercial trucks and passenger cars, each a link in a chain of destruction. The impact was deafening, a symphony of rending metal and desperate cries. Six lives were snuffed out, two of them children, their futures extinguished in the swirling grit. Eight more were hauled, broken and bleeding, to hospitals, their bodies bearing the brutal testament of the storm's fury. 

Sgt. Jay Nelson, a man who had seen two decades of Montana's harsh realities as a Highway Patrol officer, stood amidst the wreckage, his words stark and heavy. "We had an extreme weather event that was unforeseen. Based on witness reports, there was a sort of a rapidly evolving dust storm that was nearly a mile long and caused almost blackout conditions." He had never witnessed such a thing in his career, a testament to the sheer, unpredictable violence of nature. The wind, meteorologists later explained, was an outflow boundary, a sixty-mile-per-hour fist thrown by distant thunderstorms, kicking up the dry soil and turning it into a weapon. The air, thick with pulverized earth, choked engines and stole breath. 

For hours, I-90 remained a graveyard, a testament to the fragile hold humanity has on the wild. The sun, when it finally pierced the dissipating dust, cast long, accusing shadows over the mangled vehicles. The silence that followed the chaos was profound, broken only by the distant wail of sirens and the quiet, desperate work of recovery. The land, indifferent to the human tragedy it had wrought, settled back into its ancient rhythms, waiting for the next sudden, brutal shift in the wind.
